title: Shape a service Config
scenario: A tiny service needs configuration that can be owned by the service and moved between setup functions.
instructions: Define Config with owned fields: service_url: String, max_connections: usize, and use_tls: bool.
starterCode: // TODO: define Config with owned fields for the service settings.
hints: Use a struct when a few named values travel together.
String is appropriate when Config owns its service URL.
Booleans are fine for small binary flags such as TLS on or off.
completionExplanation: The Config type gives the service one explicit setup object. Owned fields make the value easy to store without tying it to temporary input lifetimes.

title: Design a parse error enum
scenario: A small user parser needs a typed error instead of string errors. Today you are defining the public error type that the rest of the parser arc will build on.
instructions: Replace the TODO with ParseUserError variants for the parser cases below: MissingId, MissingName, MissingEmail, and InvalidId.
hints: The parser already names every failure case it wants to return.
Each distinct failure mode should usually become its own enum variant.
For this first lesson, simple unit-like variants are enough; later lessons can add formatting and source errors.
completionExplanation: Each parser failure now has a named enum variant. That gives callers a typed result they can match on instead of parsing strings or comparing error messages.

title: Define an inventory item
scenario: Inventory summaries need a small domain type before helpers can work with names and quantities.
instructions: Define Item with sku: String, name: String, quantity: u32, and reserved: u32.
starterCode: // TODO: define the Item struct used by inventory summaries.
hints: Use a struct when each value has a distinct role.
The user-facing name and SKU are owned strings.
Quantities are small non-negative counts, so u32 is enough here.
completionExplanation: The Item struct gives later helpers a clear vocabulary. Named fields make summary code easier to read than tuple positions.

title: Borrow fields in a log entry
scenario: A log viewer often points into existing log text instead of allocating new strings for every parsed line.
instructions: Define LogEntry<'a> with borrowed level: &'a str and message: &'a str fields.
starterCode: // TODO: define LogEntry with borrowed fields.
hints: Borrowed struct fields need a lifetime parameter on the struct.
Both fields can borrow string slices from the original log line.
Use &'a str for each borrowed text field.
completionExplanation: Borrowed fields let the entry view point into existing text. The lifetime parameter states that the entry cannot outlive the data it references.

title: Define the Request surface
scenario: A small client library needs a request type that can be passed around after setup.
instructions: Define Request with method: String, path: String, and body: Option<String>.
starterCode: // TODO: define the Request type used by the client API.
hints: Keep the request data owned so it can outlive the builder.
The body may be absent, which makes Option a good fit.
Use clear field names that match the API vocabulary.
completionExplanation: A focused Request struct gives the API a stable output type. Owned fields keep the request independent from temporary builder input.

title: Give Config sensible defaults
scenario: Most local service runs use the same setup. Default lets callers start from a reasonable Config and override only what changes.
instructions: Implement Default for Config. Use http://localhost:8080, 32 max connections, and false for use_tls.
hints: Default returns Self from fn default() -> Self.
Use to_owned or String::from for the service URL.
Default values should be boring and safe for local development.
completionExplanation: Default gives callers a clear starting point while keeping each field explicit. It is useful when most configuration values are optional overrides.

title: Format parse errors for people
scenario: Callers can match on ParseUserError, but logs and command-line output still need clear human-readable messages.
instructions: Implement std::fmt::Display for ParseUserError. Use a match on self and write a short message for each variant.
hints: Display is implemented with fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result.
A match on self keeps one message next to each error case.
Use write!(f, "...") inside each match arm.
completionExplanation: Display separates the typed error representation from the text shown to users. The enum stays useful for programs, while fmt gives each case a stable message.

title: Collect available item names
scenario: A dashboard only needs names for items whose available count is above zero.
instructions: Implement available_names with filter_map. Include an item name only when quantity is greater than reserved.
hints: available stock is quantity minus reserved, but compare before subtracting.
filter_map can return Some(name) for included items and None for skipped items.
Borrow names with as_str instead of cloning them.
completionExplanation: filter_map fits this helper because filtering and mapping happen together. It keeps the output borrowed from the input slice instead of allocating new strings.

title: Create a lifetime-backed view
scenario: A log view should borrow a slice of entries and expose helper methods without taking ownership.
instructions: Define LogView<'a> with entries: &'a [LogEntry<'a>].
hints: The view borrows the slice, so the field starts with &'a.
The entries inside the slice also carry the same lifetime.
This is a view type, not an owning collection.
completionExplanation: The view type captures the relationship between the borrowed slice and the borrowed text inside each entry. The lifetime keeps those references tied together.

title: Add consuming builder setters
scenario: A request builder should support chained calls without exposing all its intermediate fields.
instructions: Add RequestBuilder::method(mut self, method: impl Into<String>) -> Self. Store the method and return self.
hints: Taking mut self works well for chainable builder setters.
impl Into<String> accepts both borrowed and owned string inputs.
Return self after updating the field.
completionExplanation: Consuming setters make the builder ergonomic while preserving ownership. Each call returns the next builder state for the chain.

title: Add a small Config setter
scenario: Callers should be able to start from Config::default and customize one field without reaching into every detail.
instructions: Add Config::with_service_url(self, service_url: impl Into<String>) -> Self. Update the field and return self.
hints: A consuming setter can take mut self, change a field, and return self.
impl Into<String> lets callers pass either String or &str.
Returning Self keeps the method chain-friendly.
completionExplanation: A small method can make configuration ergonomic without hiding the Config fields. Taking self by value is a common builder-style pattern.

title: Mark the parse error as an Error
scenario: The parser error now has Display. Implementing the standard Error trait lets it fit into normal Rust error handling APIs.
instructions: Add an implementation of std::error::Error for ParseUserError. No custom methods are needed yet.
hints: Error is the standard marker trait for error values.
Because Display and Debug already exist, this implementation can be empty.
The form is impl std::error::Error for ParseUserError {}.
completionExplanation: Implementing Error makes ParseUserError compatible with APIs that accept standard Rust errors. Empty implementations are common when there is no source error to expose.

title: Fold quantities into a total
scenario: Inventory reporting often needs a single total count across many items.
instructions: Implement total_quantity with iter().fold. Add each item quantity into the accumulator.
hints: fold starts with an initial accumulator value.
The accumulator and the item reference are the closure arguments.
The initial total should be 0.
completionExplanation: fold makes the accumulation explicit: start at zero, visit each item, and return the next total. It is useful when the reducer logic is more than a plain field projection.

title: Map log levels with match
scenario: The viewer needs a stable severity number for each known log level.
instructions: Add LogLevel variants Info, Warn, and Error. Implement severity with a match on level.
hints: Enums pair well with match because every known case can be handled directly.
The severity function should return a small integer for each variant.
A match expression is clearer than string comparisons here.
completionExplanation: Pattern matching keeps the mapping exhaustive and visible. Adding a new log level later will naturally point maintainers at this decision.

title: Start the builder from Default
scenario: Callers should be able to create an empty builder using the standard Default trait.
instructions: Implement Default for RequestBuilder. Both method and path should start as None.
hints: Default should produce the same empty state every time.
Option fields usually start as None in an empty builder.
You can also call Self { method: None, path: None } directly.
completionExplanation: Default makes the builder easy to construct and compose with APIs that expect defaultable values. The empty state is explicit through Option fields.

title: Model an optional timeout
scenario: Some deployments need a request timeout while others should inherit the platform default. The type should make that optionality explicit.
instructions: Add timeout_seconds: Option<u64> to Config. Use None in Default.
hints: Option<T> represents a value that may be present or absent.
A timeout expressed in seconds can be a u64.
None is clearer than a magic value such as 0 when no timeout is configured.
completionExplanation: Option makes absence part of the type instead of encoding it with a sentinel value. Callers must handle both configured and not-configured cases.

title: Choose clarity over clever chains
scenario: Iterator chains are powerful, but a dense chain can hide business rules. This helper should be easy to scan.
instructions: Refactor reorder_notes into a clearer shape. A small loop or named intermediate values are both fine if the priority rule is obvious.
starterCode:
pub fn reorder_notes(notes: &[String]) -> Vec<String> {
    notes
        .iter()
        .filter(|note| !note.trim().is_empty())
        .map(|note| note.trim().to_lowercase())
        .filter(|note| note.contains("urgent") || note.contains("stock"))
        .collect()
}
hints: The goal is not always fewer lines.
Name a boolean such as is_inventory_note if that makes the rule clearer.
A loop is idiomatic when each step deserves a name.
completionExplanation: Idiomatic Rust values clarity over using an iterator chain everywhere. The best shape is the one that makes the business rule easiest to verify.

title: Allow borrowed or owned messages
scenario: Most log messages can be borrowed, but some normalized messages need owned text. Cow supports both without forcing allocation every time.
instructions: Define LogMessage<'a> with text: Cow<'a, str>. Import Cow from std::borrow.
starterCode: // TODO: import Cow and define LogMessage with text that can be borrowed or owned.
hints: Cow means clone-on-write and lives in std::borrow.
The borrowed form for text is str, so the field type is Cow<'a, str>.
This is useful when most data can be borrowed but a few cases need ownership.
completionExplanation: Cow lets the API accept borrowed text by default while still supporting owned normalized messages. It avoids unnecessary allocation without giving up flexibility.

title: Validate builder output
scenario: A builder can be incomplete. build should report missing required fields instead of creating a bad Request.
instructions: Add BuildError variants MissingMethod and MissingPath. Change build to return Result<Request, BuildError>.
hints: The build method is where optional builder state becomes required request state.
ok_or can turn an Option into a Result with a specific error.
Each required field should have its own missing-field variant.
completionExplanation: Returning Result keeps invalid builder states from silently becoming invalid requests. Callers get a typed explanation of what still needs to be set.

title: Preserve the ID parse source
scenario: Invalid IDs come from ParseIntError. Keeping that source error helps diagnostics without giving up the typed ParseUserError API.
instructions: Change InvalidId so it stores ParseIntError, then pass the parse error through map_err instead of discarding it.
hints: Tuple variants can carry the original error value.
map_err can pass a function or variant constructor when the types line up.
After the change, InvalidId should look like InvalidId(ParseIntError).
completionExplanation: The parse error now keeps its original source. That makes the high-level error useful while preserving details for logging, debugging, or later Error::source support.

title: Return validation errors with Result
scenario: Configuration validation should explain what is wrong instead of returning a bare boolean.
instructions: Add ConfigError variants EmptyServiceUrl and ZeroConnections. Change validate to return Result<(), ConfigError>.
hints: Result<(), ConfigError> says success has no extra value, but failure is typed.
Use one variant for an empty URL and one for zero connections.
Return Ok(()) only after all validation checks pass.
completionExplanation: Result gives callers a success or a specific reason for failure. That is more useful than a boolean once validation can fail in multiple ways.

title: Convert ParseIntError with From
scenario: The parser has one low-level error that should become a ParseUserError. A From implementation makes that conversion reusable.
instructions: Implement From<ParseIntError> for ParseUserError by returning ParseUserError::InvalidId(error).
hints: From<T> defines fn from(value: T) -> Self.
The body only needs to wrap the ParseIntError in the InvalidId variant.
This is the conversion that lets ? work in later parser code.
completionExplanation: From centralizes the low-level to high-level error conversion. Call sites can ask for ParseUserError without rewriting the wrapping logic each time.

title: Make Inventory iterable
scenario: A wrapper type can expose natural iteration while still owning its internal Vec.
instructions: Implement IntoIterator for Inventory so consuming the wrapper yields Item values.
hints: The associated Item type should be Item.
Vec<Item> already has an into_iter method that consumes the vector.
The associated IntoIter type can reuse std::vec::IntoIter<Item>.
completionExplanation: IntoIterator lets Inventory participate in for loops and iterator adapters without exposing its internal field as public API.

title: Check log filtering behavior
scenario: The log viewer should keep high-priority entries and discard routine information when asked for alerts.
instructions: Fill in the TODO blocks with checks for keeping Error and Warn entries while excluding Info entries.
hints: Use a small array of LogEntry values to show each level.
The first check should prove that warning and error messages are retained.
The second check should make the Info exclusion explicit.
completionExplanation: Focused behavior checks document the alert filtering contract. They make the important inclusion and exclusion rules visible for the next reader.

title: Convert RawRequest with TryFrom
scenario: A small input DTO can be converted into a validated Request using the same fallible conversion pattern as parser code.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<RawRequest> for Request. Use BuildError as the associated error type.
hints: TryFrom is appropriate because RawRequest may be missing required data.
The associated Error type should be BuildError.
Use ok_or to extract each Option or return the matching BuildError.
completionExplanation: TryFrom turns a possibly incomplete input type into a validated domain type. The conversion API clearly communicates that failure is possible.

title: Borrow setting keys
scenario: A lookup helper should not take ownership of a key just to compare it with stored names.
instructions: Change get_setting so key is borrowed as &str and the returned value is borrowed from settings as Option<&str>.
hints: The function only needs to read key, not store it.
Returning &str can borrow from the String already inside settings.
as_str converts a String reference into a string slice.
completionExplanation: Borrowing keeps the lookup API lightweight. The caller keeps its key, and the returned value can point into the existing settings data.

title: Sort items by key
scenario: Before rendering an inventory table, the service wants stable alphabetical ordering by item name.
instructions: Implement sort_by_name with sort_by_key. Sort the mutable slice by each item's name.
hints: Slices expose in-place sorting methods.
sort_by_key takes a closure that returns the value to order by.
Borrow or clone the name as needed for the key expression.
completionExplanation: sort_by_key keeps the ordering rule close to the sort call. It is a concise fit when one field defines the order.

title: Document the request builder
scenario: The request API is small enough that one doc example can show its intended usage better than a long paragraph.
instructions: Add a short doc comment example above RequestBuilder. Show creating a builder, setting method and path, and calling build.
hints: Doc comments use /// before the item they describe.
A compact example should show the normal path, not every edge case.
Keep the example focused on how a caller should read the API.
completionExplanation: A doc example teaches the API at the point of use. It also gives future maintainers a quick reference for the intended builder flow.

title: Parse User with TryFrom
scenario: Parsing a User from text is a fallible conversion. TryFrom<&str> gives callers a standard API for that operation.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<&str> for User. Set type Error to ParseUserError and delegate to parse_user.
hints: TryFrom uses an associated Error type.
The conversion method is try_from(value: &str) -> Result<Self, Self::Error>.
You do not need to duplicate parse_user; delegate to it.
completionExplanation: TryFrom communicates that parsing can fail and gives callers a standard conversion shape. Delegating keeps one parsing implementation instead of two drifting copies.

title: Write parser behavior checks
scenario: The parser arc ends with examples that document success and failure behavior for future maintainers.
instructions: Fill in the two TODO blocks with one success check and one failure check. Use assert_eq! and matches! or a match expression.
hints: A useful success example checks the parsed id, name, and email.
A useful failure example checks the exact error variant, not just that an error happened.
matches!(result, Err(ParseUserError::MissingEmail)) is a compact shape for the failure case.
completionExplanation: Focused behavior checks turn the parser contract into executable examples for humans and future tooling. They show both the happy path and the important failure shape.

title: Hide EmailAddress internals
scenario: An email address should eventually be validated before callers can create one. Start by making the domain type own its text without exposing the field.
instructions: Replace the TODO with a private value: String field on EmailAddress.

hints.body: The field should live inside EmailAddress, but callers should not set it directly.
Use a named struct field with type String. Leave off pub so construction can be controlled later.
A canonical solution keeps the representation private.

completionExplanation: A private field lets EmailAddress own its text while reserving construction for validating APIs. That is the first step toward making invalid email values harder to represent.

title: Validate EmailAddress with TryFrom
scenario: Raw text from forms and APIs should become an EmailAddress only after validation. This is a fallible conversion.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<&str> for EmailAddress. Reject an empty string with Empty and text without @ with MissingAt.

hints.body: The conversion can fail, so the return type should be Result<Self, Self::Error>.
TryFrom has an associated Error type. Set it to EmailValidationError and return Self with a private value on success.
A canonical solution validates before constructing EmailAddress.

completionExplanation: TryFrom makes validation part of the standard conversion API. Callers can use EmailAddress::try_from or try_into without learning a custom constructor name.

title: Name the missing domain case
scenario: An address with @ but no domain should not be accepted. The error type should name that failure instead of reusing a vague variant.
instructions: Add MissingDomain to EmailValidationError and update TryFrom<&str> so text ending with @ returns that variant.

hints.body: The enum should have one variant for each failure mode callers may handle differently.
Use split_once('@') or another clear check to see whether any text exists after @.
A canonical solution adds a variant and checks the domain part before constructing the value.

completionExplanation: A distinct MissingDomain variant keeps validation errors programmatic. Callers can match the exact case without parsing message text.

title: Format EmailAddress with Display
scenario: Callers should be able to print an EmailAddress without reaching into its private field.
instructions: Implement std::fmt::Display for EmailAddress by writing the stored email text.

hints.body: Display receives &self and a formatter. It should not consume the domain value.
Use write!(f, "{}", self.value) inside fmt.
A canonical solution delegates formatting to the private string.

completionExplanation: Display exposes the public text representation without making the field public. The type keeps its invariant boundary while still fitting normal formatting APIs.

title: Format validation errors
scenario: EmailValidationError is useful to match on, but logs and UI messages need human-readable text.
instructions: Implement Display for EmailValidationError and then implement std::error::Error for it.

hints.body: Keep the enum variants programmatic. Display is where human-facing text belongs.
Match on self inside fmt. The Error implementation can be empty once Debug and Display exist.
A canonical solution gives every variant its own message and marks the enum as a standard error.

completionExplanation: Display keeps user-facing text separate from programmatic error variants. Implementing Error lets the validation error fit standard Rust error APIs.

title: Parse EmailAddress with FromStr
scenario: Once a domain type can validate &str input, callers also expect the standard string parsing API.
instructions: Implement std::str::FromStr for EmailAddress. Reuse EmailAddress::try_from instead of duplicating validation.

hints.body: FromStr is another standard fallible conversion shape for string input.
Set type Err = EmailValidationError, then delegate parse to EmailAddress::try_from.
A canonical solution keeps TryFrom as the single validation implementation.

completionExplanation: FromStr gives callers the familiar "text".parse::<EmailAddress>() API. Delegating to TryFrom avoids two validation implementations that can drift apart.

title: Define a basic Money struct
scenario: The system needs to represent monetary amounts. To prevent bugs, we must group the numeric amount and the currency type together into a single struct.
instructions: Define a public Money struct with private amount (u64) and currency (Currency) fields.

hints.body: Define pub struct Money.
The fields should be private: amount: u64 and currency: Currency. Do not add pub before them.
A canonical solution defines the Money struct with private fields.

completionExplanation: The Money struct encapsulates the amount and currency. Keeping the fields private ensures that we can control how money values are constructed and mutated.

title: Add supported Currency variants
scenario: The payment gateway is expanding. We need to support British Pounds (GBP) in our Currency representation.
instructions: Add Gbp to the Currency enum.

hints.body: Enums are updated by adding new variants separated by commas.
Add Gbp as a variant to the Currency enum.
A canonical solution adds Gbp to the enum.

completionExplanation: Updating the enum extends the supported domain concepts without altering the rest of the type structure.

title: Control Money construction invariants
scenario: To ensure money values are created consistently, we need to hide the raw fields and expose a constructor that initializes the values, along with read-only accessors.
instructions: Expose Money::new(amount: u64, currency: Currency) -> Self and accessor methods amount(&self) -> u64 and currency(&self) -> Currency.

hints.body: Write impl Money block.
new should be public and return Self. Accessors should return u64 and Currency respectively.
A canonical solution implements new and getter methods.

completionExplanation: Hiding fields and providing a constructor + accessors ensures read-only safety for users of the domain object.

title: Implement Add for same Currency
scenario: Callers need to add two money values together using the + operator. However, adding money of different currencies makes no sense and should panic, while adding the same currency should return the combined amount.
instructions: Implement std::ops::Add for Money. If the currencies do not match, panic with a clear error message.

hints.body: Import std::ops::Add, then implement Add for Money.
Check currency equality first: if self.currency != rhs.currency { panic!("...") }.
A canonical solution implements Add with a currency mismatch check.

completionExplanation: Implementing Add enables operator overloading. Panicking on currency mismatch enforces domain rule safety at runtime.

title: Convert a float string value to Money
scenario: Forms pass pricing as float-like strings (e.g. "19.99"). To avoid float rounding bugs, we must convert these strings into our integer-based Money representation fallibly.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<&str> for Money. Assume the format is a decimal number in USD. Reject invalid formats with MoneyParseError.

hints.body: Split the string on '.' to process the major and minor parts.
Convert major to cents (major * 100) and parse cents. If single-digit cents (like .2), multiply by 10.
A canonical solution parses string and validates parts.

completionExplanation: Converting string representations into integer-based pennies prevents classic float inaccuracy bugs.

title: Implement Display for Money
scenario: The UI needs to render formatted prices like $19.99 or €5.00 correctly.
instructions: Implement std::fmt::Display for Money. Use '$' for USD and '€' for EUR, and output cents formatted to 2 decimal places.

hints.body: Write impl fmt::Display for Money block.
Extract major amount (/ 100) and minor cents (% 100). Use write! with Symbol, major, and padded minor :02.
A canonical solution prints currency symbol and decimal formatting.

completionExplanation: Implementing Display gives domain objects formatting capabilities without exposing private data fields.

title: Use NonZeroU16 for Port representation
scenario: A port of 0 is invalid for our application. We can leverage Rust's standard library NonZeroU16 to enforce this rule at the compiler level.
instructions: Define a Port struct wrapping std::num::NonZeroU16 privately. Expose a constructor new returning an Option<Self>.

hints.body: Use a tuple struct: pub struct Port(NonZeroU16);
NonZeroU16::new(val) returns Option<NonZeroU16>. Map this into Port using .map(Self).
A canonical solution wraps NonZeroU16 in a tuple struct.

completionExplanation: Using NonZeroU16 prevents 0 values compile-time/construction-time, eliminating validation checks downstream.

title: Define Host domain value object
scenario: Hosts are string representations. We want to avoid using raw strings to represent hosts, so we'll wrap it in a custom type.
instructions: Define a Host struct with a private String field. Expose a Host::as_str(&self) -> &str method.

hints.body: Use a tuple struct: pub struct Host(String);
as_str should return &self.0 as a borrow string slice &str.
A canonical solution wraps String and exposes a borrow accessor.

completionExplanation: Wrapping primitive values prevents type mix-ups (like passing a email string as a host).

title: Validate Host names with TryFrom
scenario: A host must be valid. We want to reject empty hosts or hosts containing spaces.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<String> for Host. Return HostValidationError if the string is empty or contains spaces.

hints.body: Implement TryFrom<String> for Host and set type Error = HostValidationError;
Use val.is_empty() and val.contains(' ') to perform checks.
A canonical solution validates string parameters fallibly.

completionExplanation: Implementing TryFrom ensures we cannot construct invalid Host objects, maintaining structural boundaries.

title: Compose Host and Port into Endpoint
scenario: Hosts and Ports travel together as a composite network endpoint.
instructions: Define a public Endpoint struct with public host (type Host) and port (type Port) fields. Also provide a constructor Endpoint::new(host: Host, port: Port) -> Self.

hints.body: Define pub struct Endpoint with fields: pub host: Host and pub port: Port.
Implement Endpoint::new that constructs this composite type.
A canonical solution constructs composite Endpoint structs.

completionExplanation: Composing simple types makes complex domain relationships explicit and safe.

title: Give Endpoint sensible local defaults
scenario: Most service connections locally defaults to host "localhost" and port 8080.
instructions: Implement Default for Endpoint using these defaults.

hints.body: Implement Default for Endpoint and implement fn default() -> Self.
Use Host::new_unchecked("localhost".to_owned()) and Port::new(8080).unwrap() inside default().
A canonical solution provides default setups.

completionExplanation: Implementing Default facilitates rapid testing environments with predictable outcomes.

title: Implement Display for Endpoint
scenario: Connecting logs and config outputs need endpoint formatting in host:port string style.
instructions: Implement Display for Endpoint formatting it as host:port.

hints.body: Implement std::fmt::Display for Endpoint.
Use write!(f, "{}:{}", self.host.as_str(), self.port.value()) inside fmt.
A canonical solution prints host:port formatting.

completionExplanation: Implementing Display on a composite value object maps internal relationships to consistent strings.
